[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/177-Bodhran-Hinnerk-Ruemenapf-0037-p70.jpg/250px-177-Bodhran-Hinnerk-Ruemenapf-0037-p70.jpg)](/wiki/File:177-Bodhran-Hinnerk-Ruemenapf-0037-p70.jpg)

The [bodhrán](/wiki/Bodhr%C3%A1n "Bodhrán"), a traditional Irish drum

**Irish music** is music that has been created in various genres on the island of [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland").

The indigenous music of the island is termed [Irish traditional music](/wiki/Irish_traditional_music "Irish traditional music") (or Irish folk music). It has remained vibrant through the 20th and into the 21st century, despite globalising cultural forces. In spite of emigration and mass exposure to music from [Britain](/wiki/Music_of_the_United_Kingdom "Music of the United Kingdom") and the [United States](/wiki/Music_of_the_United_States "Music of the United States"), Ireland's traditional music has kept many of its elements and has itself influenced other forms of music, such as [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") and [roots music](/wiki/American_folk_music "American folk music") in the United States, which in turn have had some influence on modern [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"). Irish folk music has occasionally been fused with [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock"), [electronic rock](/wiki/Electronic_rock "Electronic rock") and other genres. Some of these fusion artists have attained mainstream success, at home and abroad.

In art music, Ireland has a history reaching back to [Gregorian chants](/wiki/Gregorian_chant "Gregorian chant") in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), [choral](/wiki/Choir "Choir") and [harp](/wiki/Harp "Harp") [music of the Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_music "Renaissance music"), court [music of the Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music") and early [Classical period](/wiki/Classical_period_(music) "Classical period (music)"), as well as many [Romantic](/wiki/Romantic_music "Romantic music"), late Romantic and twentieth-century [modernist music](/wiki/Modernism_(music) "Modernism (music)"). It is still a vibrant genre with many composers and ensembles writing and performing [avant-garde art music](/wiki/Avant-garde_music "Avant-garde music") in the classical tradition.

On a smaller scale, Ireland has also produced many [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") musicians of note, particularly after the 1950s.

## Early Irish music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early Irish music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/8b/Rosgall.jpg/250px-Rosgall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rosgall.jpg)

A 16th century [Irish Warpipe](/wiki/Great_Irish_Warpipes "Great Irish Warpipes") player

By the High and Late Medieval Era, the [Irish annals](/wiki/Irish_annals "Irish annals") were listing native musicians, such as the following:

* *921BC: Cú Congalta, priest of Lann-Leire, the Tethra (i. e. the singer or orator) for voice, personal form and knowledge, died.*
* *1011: Connmhach Ua Tomhrair, priest and chief singer of [Cluain-mic-Nois](/wiki/Clonmacnoise "Clonmacnoise"), died.*
* *1168: [Amhlaeibh Mac Innaighneorach](/wiki/Amhlaeibh_Mac_Innaighneorach "Amhlaeibh Mac Innaighneorach"), chief ollamh of Ireland in harp-playing, died.*
* *1226: [Aed mac Donn Ó Sochlachain](/wiki/Aed_mac_Donn_%C3%93_Sochlachain "Aed mac Donn Ó Sochlachain"), erenagh of [Cong](/wiki/Cong,_County_Mayo "Cong, County Mayo"), a man eminent for chanting and for the right tuning of harps and for having made an instrument for himself which none had made before, distinguished also in every art such as poetry, engraving and writing and in every skilled occupation, sadly died.*
* *1269: [Aed Ó Finn](/wiki/Aed_%C3%93_Finn "Aed Ó Finn"), master of music and minstrelsy, died.*
* *1329: [Maol Ruanaidh Cam Ó Cearbhaill](/wiki/Maol_Ruanaidh_Cam_%C3%93_Cearbhaill "Maol Ruanaidh Cam Ó Cearbhaill"), [tiompanist](/wiki/Tiompan "Tiompan"), murdered during the Braganstown Massacre in [County Louth](/wiki/County_Louth "County Louth").*
* *1330: [Mael Sechlainn Mac Carmaic](/wiki/Mael_Sechlainn_Mac_Carmaic "Mael Sechlainn Mac Carmaic"), a general entertainer, died.*
* *1343: [Donnchad Clereach Ó Maol Braonáin](/wiki/Donnchad_Clereach_%C3%93_Maol_Braon%C3%A1in "Donnchad Clereach Ó Maol Braonáin"), a [choral](/wiki/Choral "Choral") [canon](/wiki/Canon_(priest) "Canon (priest)") of [Elphin](/wiki/Elphin,_County_Roscommon "Elphin, County Roscommon"), was killed by an arrow.*
* *1357: [Donn Shléibhe Mac Cerbaill](/wiki/Donn_Shl%C3%A9ibhe_Mac_Cerbaill "Donn Shléibhe Mac Cerbaill"), an accomplished musician ... died.*
* *1360: [Gilla na Naem Ó Conmaigh](/wiki/Gilla_na_Naem_%C3%93_Conmaigh "Gilla na Naem Ó Conmaigh"), music [ollamh](/wiki/Ollam "Ollam") of [Thomond](/wiki/Thomond "Thomond") ... died.*
* *1361. [Magraith Ó Fionnachta](/wiki/Magraith_%C3%93_Fionnachta "Magraith Ó Fionnachta"), Chief Musician and [Tiompanist](/wiki/Tiompan "Tiompan") to the [Síol Muireadaigh](/wiki/S%C3%ADol_Muireadaigh "Síol Muireadaigh"), died.*
* *1364: Bran Ó Brain, a skilful tympanist ... died.*
* *1369: John Mac Egan, and Gilbert Ó Bardan, two accomplished young harpers of [Conmaicne](/wiki/Conmaicne "Conmaicne"), died.*
* *1469: Ruaidrí mac Donnchad Ó Dálaigh, the most musical-handed harpist in all Ireland.*
* *1490: Diarmait MacCairbre, harper, was executed.*
* *1553: Tadhg, son of Ruaidhri Ó Comhdhain, i.e. the ollamh of [Éire](/wiki/%C3%89ire "Éire") and [Alba](/wiki/Alba "Alba") in music, died.*
* *1561: [Naisse mac Cithruadh](/wiki/Naisse_mac_Cithruadh "Naisse mac Cithruadh"), drowned on [Lough Gill](/wiki/Lough_Gill "Lough Gill").*
* *1589. Daighre [Ó Duibhgeannáin](/wiki/%C3%93_Duibhgeann%C3%A1in "Ó Duibhgeannáin"), a most affable, musical man, died.*

### Modern interpretation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Modern interpretation")]

Early Irish poetry and song has been translated into modern Irish and English by notable Irish poets, song collectors and musicians.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The 6th century hymn *Rop tú mo baile* by [Dallán Forgaill](/wiki/Dall%C3%A1n_Forgaill "Dallán Forgaill") for example, was published in 1905 in English by [Mary Elizabeth Byrne](/wiki/Mary_Elizabeth_Byrne "Mary Elizabeth Byrne"), and is widely known as *[Be Thou My Vision](/wiki/Be_Thou_My_Vision "Be Thou My Vision")*. *The Blackbird of Belfast Lough* ([Old Irish](/wiki/Old_Irish_language "Old Irish language"): *Int én bec*; [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"): *An t-éan beag*) has been notably translated by poets such as [Seamus Heaney](/wiki/Seamus_Heaney "Seamus Heaney"), [Ciaran Carson](/wiki/Ciaran_Carson "Ciaran Carson") and [Frank O'Connor](/wiki/Frank_O%27Connor "Frank O'Connor"). Notable recordings of modern interpretations of early Irish music include [Pádraigín Ní Uallacháin](/wiki/P%C3%A1draig%C3%ADn_N%C3%AD_Uallach%C3%A1in "Pádraigín Ní Uallacháin")'s *[Songs of the Scribe](/wiki/Songs_of_the_Scribe "Songs of the Scribe")*, various music albums by choral group [Anúna](/wiki/An%C3%BAna "Anúna"), and the recordings of [Caitríona O'Leary](/wiki/Caitr%C3%ADona_O%27Leary "Caitríona O'Leary") with Dúlra and the eX Ensemble.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Early Irish musicians abroad

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early Irish musicians abroad")]

Some musicians were acclaimed in places beyond Ireland. [Cú Chuimne](/wiki/C%C3%BA_Chuimne "Cú Chuimne") (died 747) lived much of his adult life in Gaelic Scotland, and composed at least one hymn. [Foillan](/wiki/Foillan "Foillan"), who was alive in the seventh century, travelled through much of Britain and France; around 653 at the request of [Saint Gertrude of Brabant](/wiki/Gertrude_of_Nivelles "Gertrude of Nivelles"), taught [psalmody](/wiki/Psalmody "Psalmody") to her nuns at Nievelle. [Tuotilo](/wiki/Tuotilo "Tuotilo") (c.850–c. 915), who lived in Italy and Germany, was noted both as a musician and a composer.

[Helias of Cologne](/wiki/Helias_of_Cologne "Helias of Cologne") (died 1040), is held to be the first to introduce [Roman chant](/wiki/Roman_chant "Roman chant") to [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"). His contemporary, [Aaron Scotus](/wiki/Aaron_Scotus "Aaron Scotus") (died 18 November 1052) was an acclaimed composer of [Gregorian chant](/wiki/Gregorian_chant "Gregorian chant") in Germany.

[Donell Dubh Ó Cathail](/wiki/Donell_Dubh_%C3%93_Cathail "Donell Dubh Ó Cathail") (c. 1560s-c.1660), was not only musician of [Viscount Buttevant](/wiki/Viscount_Buttevant "Viscount Buttevant"), but, with his uncle [Donell Óge Ó Cathail](/w/index.php?title=Donell_%C3%93ge_%C3%93_Cathail&action=edit&redlink=1 "Donell Óge Ó Cathail (page does not exist)"), harper to [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England").

## Early modern times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early modern times")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

[Give Me Your Hand](/wiki/File:Dancing_Willow_-_Demo-CD_2007_06_-_Give_me_your_hand.ogg "File:Dancing Willow - Demo-CD 2007 06 - Give me your hand.ogg")

Instrumental featuring viola da gamba and recorder, performed by Dancing Willow

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

Up to the seventeenth century, harp musicians were patronised by the aristocracy in Ireland. This tradition died out in the eighteenth century with the collapse of [Gaelic Ireland](/wiki/Gaelic_Ireland "Gaelic Ireland"). [Turlough Carolan](/wiki/Turlough_Carolan "Turlough Carolan") (1670–1738) is the best known of those harpists,[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) and over 200 of his compositions are known. Some of his pieces use elements of contemporary baroque music, but his music has entered the tradition and is played by many folk musicians today. [Edward Bunting](/wiki/Edward_Bunting "Edward Bunting") collected some of the last-known Irish harp tunes at the [Belfast Harp Festival](/wiki/Belfast_Harp_Festival "Belfast Harp Festival") in 1792. Other important collectors of Irish music include [Francis O'Neill](/wiki/Francis_O%27Neill "Francis O'Neill")[[5]](#cite_note-5) and [George Petrie](/wiki/George_Petrie_(antiquarian) "George Petrie (antiquarian)").

Other notable Irish musicians of this era included [Cearbhall Óg Ó Dálaigh](/wiki/Cearbhall_%C3%93g_%C3%93_D%C3%A1laigh "Cearbhall Óg Ó Dálaigh") (fl. c. 1630); [Piaras Feiritéar](/wiki/Piaras_Feirit%C3%A9ar "Piaras Feiritéar") (1600?–1653); [William Connellan](/wiki/William_Connellan "William Connellan") (fl. mid-17th century) and his brother, [Thomas Connellan](/wiki/Thomas_Connellan "Thomas Connellan") (c. 1640/1645–1698), composers; [Dominic Ó Mongain](/wiki/Dominic_%C3%93_Mongain "Dominic Ó Mongain") (alive 18th century); [Donnchadh Ó Hámsaigh](/wiki/Donnchadh_%C3%93_H%C3%A1msaigh "Donnchadh Ó Hámsaigh") (1695–1807); poet and songwriter [Eoghan Rua Ó Súilleabháin](/wiki/Eoghan_Rua_%C3%93_S%C3%BAilleabh%C3%A1in "Eoghan Rua Ó Súilleabháin") (1748–1782); [Arthur O'Neill](/wiki/Arthur_O%27Neill_(harpist) "Arthur O'Neill (harpist)") (fl. 1792); [Patrick Byrne](/wiki/Patrick_Byrne_(musician) "Patrick Byrne (musician)") (c.1794–1863); world-renowned piper [Tarlach Mac Suibhne](/wiki/Tarlach_Mac_Suibhne "Tarlach Mac Suibhne") (c. 1831–1916); poet and songwriter [Colm de Bhailís](/wiki/Colm_de_Bhail%C3%ADs "Colm de Bhailís") (1796–1906).

## Traditional music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Traditional music")]

Main article: [Irish traditional music](/wiki/Irish_traditional_music "Irish traditional music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Dungloe_music_festival_%281%29_-_geograph.org.uk_-_51567.jpg/250px-Dungloe_music_festival_%281%29_-_geograph.org.uk_-_51567.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dungloe_music_festival_(1)_-_geograph.org.uk_-_51567.jpg)

A traditional music session, known in some circles in [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language") as a *seisiún*, a word invented in the 1990s

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Ashbury_Irish_Bouzouki_Front.jpg/250px-Ashbury_Irish_Bouzouki_Front.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ashbury_Irish_Bouzouki_Front.jpg)

An [Irish bouzouki](/wiki/Irish_bouzouki "Irish bouzouki")

Irish traditional music includes many kinds of songs, including drinking songs, [ballads](/wiki/Ballad "Ballad") and [laments](/wiki/Lament "Lament"), sung unaccompanied or with accompaniment by a variety of instruments. Traditional [dance music](/wiki/Dance_music "Dance music") includes [reels](/wiki/Reel_(dance) "Reel (dance)") (**4**  
**4**), [hornpipes](/wiki/Hornpipe "Hornpipe") and [jigs](/wiki/Jig "Jig") (the common double jig is in **6**  
**8** time).[[6]](#cite_note-6) The [polka](/wiki/Polka "Polka") arrived at the start of the nineteenth century, spread by itinerant dancing masters and mercenary soldiers, returning from Europe.[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Set dancing](/wiki/Set_dancing "Set dancing") may have arrived in the eighteenth century.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Later imported dance-signatures include the [mazurka](/wiki/Mazurka "Mazurka") and the highlands (a sort of Irished version of the Scottish [strathspey](/wiki/Strathspey_(dance) "Strathspey (dance)")).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The [Irish fiddle](/wiki/Irish_fiddle "Irish fiddle") was said by one nationalist researcher to have been played in Ireland since the 8th century, although this has never been proved by texts or artifacts.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The [bagpipes](/wiki/Bagpipes "Bagpipes") have a long history of being associated with Ireland [Great Irish warpipes](/wiki/Great_Irish_warpipes "Great Irish warpipes") were once commonly used in Ireland especially in battle as far back as the 15th century.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

A revival of Irish traditional music took place around the turn of the 20th century. The button [accordion](/wiki/Accordion "Accordion") and the [concertina](/wiki/Concertina "Concertina") were becoming common.[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Irish stepdance](/wiki/Irish_stepdance "Irish stepdance") was performed at [céilís](/wiki/C%C3%A9ilidh "Céilidh"), organised competitions and at some country houses where local and itinerant musicians were welcome.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Irish dancing was supported by the educational system and patriotic organisations. An older style of singing called *[sean-nós](/wiki/Sean-n%C3%B3s_singing "Sean-nós singing")* ("in the old style"), which is a form of [traditional Irish singing](/wiki/Traditional_Irish_singing "Traditional Irish singing") was still found, mainly for very poetic songs in the [Irish language](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

From 1820 to 1920 over 4,400,000 Irish emigrated to the US, creating an Irish diaspora in Philadelphia, Chicago (see [Francis O'Neill](/wiki/Francis_O%27Neill "Francis O'Neill")), Boston, New York and other cities.[[15]](#cite_note-15) O'Neill made the first recordings of Irish music on [Edison](/wiki/Edison_Records "Edison Records") wax cylinders.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Later, Irish musicians who were successful in the USA made commercial recordings which found their way around the world and re-invigorated musical styles back in the homeland.[[17]](#cite_note-17) For example, American-based fiddlers like [Michael Coleman](/wiki/Michael_Coleman_(Irish_musician) "Michael Coleman (Irish musician)"), [James Morrison](/wiki/James_Morrison_(fiddler) "James Morrison (fiddler)") and [Paddy Killoran](/wiki/Paddy_Killoran "Paddy Killoran") did much to popularise Irish music in the 1920s and 1930s, while [Ed Reavy](/wiki/Ed_Reavy "Ed Reavy") composed over a hundred tunes that have since entered the tradition in both Ireland and the diaspora.

Brian Boru's March with traditional flute performed by the U.S. Marine Band

After a lull in the 1940s and 1950s, when (except for [Céilidh bands](/wiki/C%C3%A9ilidh "Céilidh")) traditional music was at a low ebb, [Seán Ó Riada](/wiki/Se%C3%A1n_%C3%93_Riada "Seán Ó Riada")'s [Ceoltóirí Chualann](/wiki/Ceolt%C3%B3ir%C3%AD_Chualann "Ceoltóirí Chualann"), [The Chieftains](/wiki/The_Chieftains "The Chieftains"), [Tom Lenihan](/wiki/Tom_Lenihan "Tom Lenihan"), [The Clancy Brothers and Tommy Makem](/wiki/The_Clancy_Brothers_and_Tommy_Makem "The Clancy Brothers and Tommy Makem"), [The Irish Rovers](/wiki/The_Irish_Rovers "The Irish Rovers"), [The Dubliners](/wiki/The_Dubliners "The Dubliners"), [Ryan's Fancy](/wiki/Ryan%27s_Fancy "Ryan's Fancy") and [Sweeney's Men](/wiki/Sweeney%27s_Men "Sweeney's Men") were in large part responsible for a second wave of revitalisation of Irish folk music in the 1960s. Several of these were featured in the 2010 TV movie "My Music: When Irish Eyes are Smiling".[[18]](#cite_note-18) Seán Ó Riada in particular was singled out as a force who did much for Irish music, through programming on Radio Éireann in the late 1940s through the 1960s. He worked to promote and encourage the performing of traditional Irish music, and his work as a promoter and performer led directly to the formation of the Chieftains. His work inspired the likes of [Planxty](/wiki/Planxty "Planxty"), [The Bothy Band](/wiki/The_Bothy_Band "The Bothy Band") and [Clannad](/wiki/Clannad_(musical_group) "Clannad (musical group)") in the 70s. Later came such bands as [Stockton's Wing](/wiki/Stockton%27s_Wing "Stockton's Wing"), [De Dannan](/wiki/De_Dannan "De Dannan"), [Altan](/wiki/Altan_(band) "Altan (band)"), [Arcady](/w/index.php?title=Arcady_(band)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Arcady (band) (page does not exist)"), [Dervish](/wiki/Dervish_(band) "Dervish (band)") and [Patrick Street](/wiki/Patrick_Street "Patrick Street"), along with a wealth of individual performers.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

More and more people play Irish music and new bands emerge every year such as [Téada](/wiki/T%C3%A9ada "Téada"), [Gráda](/wiki/Gr%C3%A1da "Gráda"), [Dervish](/wiki/Dervish_(band) "Dervish (band)"), and [Lúnasa](/wiki/L%C3%BAnasa_(band) "Lúnasa (band)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Classical music in Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Classical music in Ireland")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/John_Field_-_restoration1.jpg/250px-John_Field_-_restoration1.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Field_-_restoration1.jpg)

[John Field](/wiki/John_Field_(composer) "John Field (composer)"), one of Ireland's foremost classical composers

There is evidence of music in the "classical" tradition since the early 15th century when a polyphonic choir was established at Christ Church Cathedral, Dublin, and "city musicians" were employed in the major cities and towns, who performed on festive occasions. In the 18th century, Dublin was known as the "Second City" of the British Isles, with an active musical life culminating in, among other events, the first performance of [Handel](/wiki/George_Frideric_Handel "George Frideric Handel")'s famous oratorio *[Messiah](/wiki/Messiah_(Handel) "Messiah (Handel)")*. The [ballad opera](/wiki/Ballad_opera "Ballad opera") trend, caused by the success of the [Beggar's Opera](/wiki/Beggar%27s_Opera "Beggar's Opera"), has left noticeable traces in Ireland, with many works that influenced the genre in England and on the continent, by musicians such as [Charles Coffey](/wiki/Charles_Coffey "Charles Coffey") and [Kane O'Hara](/wiki/Kane_O%27Hara "Kane O'Hara").

### Composers of note

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Composers of note")]

Apart from the harper-composers of the 16th century, composers in the 16th and 17th century usually came from a Protestant Anglo-Irish background, as due to the discrimination of Catholics no formal musical education was available to them. Composers were often associated with either [Dublin Castle](/wiki/Dublin_Castle "Dublin Castle") or one of the Dublin cathedrals ([St Patrick's](/wiki/St_Patrick%27s_Cathedral,_Dublin "St Patrick's Cathedral, Dublin") and [Christ Church](/wiki/Christ_Church_Cathedral,_Dublin "Christ Church Cathedral, Dublin")). These include immigrants in the 18th century such as [Johann Sigismund Cousser](/wiki/Johann_Sigismund_Kusser "Johann Sigismund Kusser"), [Matthew Dubourg](/wiki/Matthew_Dubourg "Matthew Dubourg"), and [Tommaso Giordani](/wiki/Tommaso_Giordani "Tommaso Giordani"). [Thomas Roseingrave](/wiki/Thomas_Roseingrave "Thomas Roseingrave") and his brother Ralph were prominent Irish baroque composers. Among the next generation of composers were the Cork-born [Philip Cogan](/wiki/Philip_Cogan "Philip Cogan") (1750–1833), a prominent composer of piano music including concertos, [John Andrew Stevenson](/wiki/John_Andrew_Stevenson "John Andrew Stevenson") (1761–1833), who is best known for his publications of *Irish Melodies* with poet [Thomas Moore](/wiki/Thomas_Moore "Thomas Moore"), who also wrote operas, religious music, catches, glees, odes, and songs. In the early 19th century Irish-born composers dominated English-language opera in England and Ireland, including [Charles Thomas Carter](/wiki/Charles_Thomas_Carter "Charles Thomas Carter") (c.1735–1804), [Michael Kelly](/wiki/Michael_Kelly_(tenor) "Michael Kelly (tenor)") (1762–1826), [Thomas Simpson Cooke](/wiki/Thomas_Simpson_Cooke "Thomas Simpson Cooke") (1782–1848), [William Henry Kearns](/wiki/William_Henry_Kearns "William Henry Kearns") (1794–1846), [Joseph Augustine Wade](/wiki/Joseph_Augustine_Wade "Joseph Augustine Wade") (1801–1845) and, later in the century, [Michael W. Balfe](/wiki/Michael_W._Balfe "Michael W. Balfe") (1808–1870) and [William Vincent Wallace](/wiki/William_Vincent_Wallace "William Vincent Wallace") (1812–1865). [John Field](/wiki/John_Field_(composer) "John Field (composer)") (1782–1837) has been credited with the creation of the Nocturne form, which influenced [Frédéric Chopin](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Chopin "Frédéric Chopin"). [John William Glover](/wiki/John_William_Glover "John William Glover") (1815–1899), [Joseph Robinson](/wiki/Joseph_Robinson_(composer) "Joseph Robinson (composer)") (1815–1898) and [Robert Prescott Stewart](/wiki/Robert_Prescott_Stewart "Robert Prescott Stewart") (1825–1894) kept Irish classical music in Dublin alive in the 19th century, while mid-19th-century emigrants include [George William Torrance](/wiki/George_William_Torrance "George William Torrance") and [George Alexander Osborne](/wiki/George_Alexander_Osborne "George Alexander Osborne"). [Charles Villiers Stanford](/wiki/Charles_Villiers_Stanford "Charles Villiers Stanford") (1852–1924) and [Hamilton Harty](/wiki/Hamilton_Harty "Hamilton Harty") (1879–1941) were among the last emigrants in Irish music, combining a late romantic musical language with Irish folklorism. Their contemporary in Ireland was the Italian immigrant [Michele Esposito](/wiki/Michele_Esposito "Michele Esposito") (1855–1929), a figure of seminal importance in Irish music who arrived in Ireland in 1882. The years after Irish independence were a difficult period in which composers tried to find an identifiable Irish voice in an anti-British climate, which included ressentiments against classical music as such. The development of Irish broadcasting in the 1920s and the gradual enlargement of the Radio Éireann Orchestra in the late 1930s improved the situation. Important composers in these years were [John F. Larchet](/wiki/John_F._Larchet "John F. Larchet") (1884–1967), [Ina Boyle](/wiki/Ina_Boyle "Ina Boyle") (1889–1967), [Arthur Duff](/wiki/Arthur_Duff_(composer) "Arthur Duff (composer)") (1899–1956), [Aloys Fleischmann](/wiki/Aloys_Fleischmann "Aloys Fleischmann") (1910–1992), [Frederick May](/wiki/Frederick_May_(composer) "Frederick May (composer)") (1911–1985), [Joan Trimble](/wiki/Joan_Trimble "Joan Trimble") (1915–2000), and [Brian Boydell](/wiki/Brian_Boydell "Brian Boydell") (1917–2000). The middle decades of the 20th century were also shaped by [A.J. Potter](/wiki/A.J._Potter "A.J. Potter") (1918–1980), [Gerard Victory](/wiki/Gerard_Victory "Gerard Victory") (1921–1995), [James Wilson](/wiki/James_Wilson_(composer) "James Wilson (composer)") (1922–2005), [Seán Ó Riada](/wiki/Se%C3%A1n_%C3%93_Riada "Seán Ó Riada") (1931–1971), [John Kinsella](/wiki/John_Kinsella_(composer) "John Kinsella (composer)") (1932–2021), and [Seóirse Bodley](/wiki/Se%C3%B3irse_Bodley "Seóirse Bodley") (1933–2023). Prominent names among the older generation of composers in Ireland today are [Frank Corcoran](/wiki/Frank_Corcoran "Frank Corcoran") (b. 1944), [Eric Sweeney](/wiki/Eric_Sweeney_(composer) "Eric Sweeney (composer)") (1948–2020), [John Buckley](/wiki/John_Buckley_(composer) "John Buckley (composer)") (b. 1951), [Gerald Barry](/wiki/Gerald_Barry_(composer) "Gerald Barry (composer)") (b. 1952), [Raymond Deane](/wiki/Raymond_Deane "Raymond Deane") (b. 1953), [Gearóid Ó Deaghaidh](/w/index.php?title=Gear%C3%B3id_%C3%93_Deaghaidh_(composer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gearóid Ó Deaghaidh (composer) (page does not exist)") (b. 1954),[[20]](#cite_note-20) [Patrick Cassidy](/wiki/Patrick_Cassidy_(composer) "Patrick Cassidy (composer)") (b. 1956), and [Fergus Johnston](/wiki/Fergus_Johnston "Fergus Johnston") (b. 1959) (see also [List of Irish classical composers](/wiki/List_of_Irish_classical_composers "List of Irish classical composers")).

### Performers of note

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Performers of note")]

Performers of note in classical music include [Catherine Hayes](/wiki/Catherine_Hayes_(soprano) "Catherine Hayes (soprano)") (1818–1861), Ireland's first great international *prima donna* and the first Irish woman to perform at La Scala in Milan; tenor [Barton McGuckin](/wiki/Barton_McGuckin "Barton McGuckin") (1852–1913), a much-demanded singer in the late 19th century; tenor [Joseph O'Mara](/wiki/Joseph_O%27Mara "Joseph O'Mara") (1864–1927), a very prominent singer around the turn of the century; tenor [John McCormack](/wiki/John_McCormack_(tenor) "John McCormack (tenor)") (1884–1945), the most celebrated tenor of his day; opera singer [Margaret Burke-Sheridan](/wiki/Margaret_Burke-Sheridan "Margaret Burke-Sheridan") (1889–1958); pianist [Charles Lynch](/wiki/Charles_Lynch_(pianist) "Charles Lynch (pianist)") (1906–1984); tenor [Josef Locke](/wiki/Josef_Locke "Josef Locke") (1917–1999) achieved global success and was the subject of the 1991 film *[Hear My Song](/wiki/Hear_My_Song "Hear My Song")*; the concert flautist [Sir James Galway](/wiki/Sir_James_Galway "Sir James Galway") and pianist [Barry Douglas](/wiki/Barry_Douglas_(musician) "Barry Douglas (musician)").[[21]](#cite_note-grove-21) Douglas achieved fame in 1986 by claiming the [International Tchaikovsky Competition](/wiki/International_Tchaikovsky_Competition "International Tchaikovsky Competition") gold medal. Mezzo-sopranos [Bernadette Greevy](/wiki/Bernadette_Greevy "Bernadette Greevy") and [Ann Murray](/wiki/Ann_Murray "Ann Murray") have also had success internationally.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Choral music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Choral music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Anuna.jpg/250px-Anuna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anuna.jpg)

Anúna

Choral music has been practised in Ireland for centuries, initially at the larger churches such as [Christ Church Cathedral](/wiki/Christ_Church_Cathedral,_Dublin "Christ Church Cathedral, Dublin"), [St Patrick's Cathedral](/wiki/St_Patrick%27s_Cathedral,_Dublin "St Patrick's Cathedral, Dublin"), and [St Mary's Cathedral](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Cathedral,_Dublin "St Mary's Cathedral, Dublin"), as well as the University of Dublin Choral Society (founded in 1837).

Founded and directed by composer [Michael McGlynn](/wiki/Michael_McGlynn "Michael McGlynn") in 1987, [Anúna](/wiki/An%C3%BAna "Anúna") contributed significantly to raising the profile of choral music, particularly through their contributions to [Riverdance](/wiki/Riverdance "Riverdance") which they were a part of from 1994 to 1996. They were nominated for a Classical Brit Award in the UK and appeared at the [BBC Proms](/wiki/BBC_Proms "BBC Proms") series in the [Royal Albert Hall](/wiki/Royal_Albert_Hall "Royal Albert Hall") in 1999. In 2012 they featured as the voices of Hell in the video game [Diablo III](/wiki/Diablo_III "Diablo III").[[23]](#cite_note-23) In February 2018 the group won the Outstanding Ensemble category of the Annual Game Music Awards 2017 for their contributions to the video game [Xenoblade Chronicles 2](/wiki/Xenoblade_Chronicles_2 "Xenoblade Chronicles 2").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The [Chamber Choir Ireland](/wiki/Chamber_Choir_Ireland "Chamber Choir Ireland"), formerly National Chamber Choir of Ireland, is principally funded by the [Arts Council of Ireland](/wiki/Arts_Council_of_Ireland "Arts Council of Ireland"). Their artistic director is [Paul Hillier](/wiki/Paul_Hillier "Paul Hillier").[[25]](#cite_note-25) The choir has produced a number of CDs with international (including Irish) repertoire. There are many semi-professional choirs in Ireland at local level, too. Many perform and compete at the annual [Cork International Choral Festival](/wiki/Cork_International_Choral_Festival "Cork International Choral Festival") (since 1954).

### Opera

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Opera")]

Although Ireland had no purpose-built opera house for a long time, opera has been performed in Ireland since the 17th century. In the 18th century, Ireland was a centre for [ballad opera](/wiki/Ballad_opera "Ballad opera") and created important works that helped to develop the genre in the direction of operetta, with works by [Charles Coffey](/wiki/Charles_Coffey "Charles Coffey") and [Kane O'Hara](/wiki/Kane_O%27Hara "Kane O'Hara"). Nationally identifiable Irish operas have been written by immigrants such as [Tommaso Giordani](/wiki/Tommaso_Giordani "Tommaso Giordani") and [Johann Bernhard Logier](/wiki/Johann_Bernhard_Logier "Johann Bernhard Logier") as well as by native composers such as [John Andrew Stevenson](/wiki/John_Andrew_Stevenson "John Andrew Stevenson") and [Thomas Simpson Cooke](/wiki/Thomas_Simpson_Cooke "Thomas Simpson Cooke"), continued in the 19th century with works by [John William Glover](/wiki/John_William_Glover "John William Glover") and [Paul McSwiney](/wiki/Paul_McSwiney "Paul McSwiney").[[26]](#cite_note-26) [Michael William Balfe](/wiki/Michael_William_Balfe "Michael William Balfe") and [Vincent Wallace](/wiki/Vincent_Wallace "Vincent Wallace") were the most prominent representatives of mid-19th-century English-language operas.

The Celtic Renaissance after 1900 created works such as *[Muirgheis](/wiki/Muirgheis "Muirgheis")* (1903) by [Thomas O'Brien Butler](/wiki/Thomas_O%27Brien_Butler "Thomas O'Brien Butler"), *Connla of the Golden Hair* (1903) by William Harvey Pélissier, *[Eithne](/wiki/Eithne_(opera) "Eithne (opera)")* (1909) by [Robert O'Dwyer](/wiki/Robert_O%27Dwyer "Robert O'Dwyer"), and *The Tinker and the Fairy* (1910) by [Michele Esposito](/wiki/Michele_Esposito "Michele Esposito"). *Muirgheis* and *Eithne* have librettos in Irish, as have a number of works by [Geoffrey Molyneux Palmer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Molyneux_Palmer "Geoffrey Molyneux Palmer") and several 1940s and '50s works by [Éamonn Ó Gallchobhair](/wiki/%C3%89amonn_%C3%93_Gallchobhair "Éamonn Ó Gallchobhair"). Most of the Irish operas written since the 1960s have a contemporary international outlook, with important works by [Gerard Victory](/wiki/Gerard_Victory "Gerard Victory"), [James Wilson](/wiki/James_Wilson_(composer) "James Wilson (composer)"), [Raymond Deane](/wiki/Raymond_Deane "Raymond Deane"), [Gerald Barry](/wiki/Gerald_Barry_(composer) "Gerald Barry (composer)"), and a number of young composers since the turn of the century.

There have been subsequent attempts to revive the Irish-language tradition in opera. A brother-sister team previewed sections of the opera *Clann Tuireann* publicly.[[27]](#cite_note-27) In 2024 musician and composer [John Spillane](/wiki/John_Spillane "John Spillane") premiered his bilingual opera *Fíoruisce - The Legend of the Lough*.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

[Wexford Festival Opera](/wiki/Wexford_Festival_Opera "Wexford Festival Opera") is a major international festival that takes place every October and November.

## Popular music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Popular music")]

### Early popular performers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Early popular performers")]

Performers of popular music began appearing as early as the late 1940s; [Delia Murphy](/wiki/Delia_Murphy "Delia Murphy") popularised Irish folk songs that she recorded for [His Master's Voice](/wiki/His_Master%27s_Voice_(British_record_label) "His Master's Voice (British record label)") in 1949; [Margaret Barry](/wiki/Margaret_Barry "Margaret Barry") is also credited with bringing traditional songs to the fore; Donegal's [Bridie Gallagher](/wiki/Bridie_Gallagher "Bridie Gallagher") shot to fame in 1956 and is considered 'Ireland's first international pop star';[[30]](#cite_note-30) Belfast-born singer [Ruby Murray](/wiki/Ruby_Murray "Ruby Murray") achieved unprecedented chart success in the UK in the mid-1950s; Dublin native [Carmel Quinn](/wiki/Carmel_Quinn "Carmel Quinn") emigrated to the US and became a regular singer on [Arthur Godfrey's Talent Scouts](/wiki/Arthur_Godfrey%27s_Talent_Scouts "Arthur Godfrey's Talent Scouts") and appeared frequently on other TV variety shows in the 1950s and '60s. [The Bachelors](/wiki/The_Bachelors "The Bachelors") were an all-male harmony group from Dublin who had hits in the UK, Europe, US, Australia and Russia; [Mary O'Hara](/wiki/Mary_O%27Hara "Mary O'Hara") was a soprano and harpist who was successful on both sides of the Atlantic in the 1950s and early 1960s; Waterford crooner [Val Doonican](/wiki/Val_Doonican "Val Doonican") had a string of UK hits and presented his own TV show on the BBC from 1965 to 1986.

### Showbands in Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Showbands in Ireland")]

Main article: [Irish showband](/wiki/Irish_showband "Irish showband")

Irish [Showbands](/wiki/Irish_showband "Irish showband") were a major force in Irish popular music, particularly in rural areas, for twenty years from the mid-1950s. The showband played in dance halls and was loosely based on the six or seven piece [Dixieland](/wiki/Dixieland "Dixieland") dance band. The basic showband repertoire included standard dance numbers, [cover versions](/wiki/Cover_versions "Cover versions") of pop music hits, ranging from [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll"), [country and western](/wiki/Country_and_western "Country and western") to [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") standards. Key to the showband's success was the ability to learn and perform songs currently in the [record charts](/wiki/Record_chart "Record chart"). They sometimes played [Irish traditional](/wiki/Folk_music_of_Ireland "Folk music of Ireland") or [Céilidh music](/wiki/C%C3%A9ilidh "Céilidh") and a few included self-composed songs.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Country and Irish

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Country and Irish")]

Main article: [Country and Irish](/wiki/Country_and_Irish "Country and Irish")

With the rise in popularity of [American country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"), a new subgenre developed in Ireland known as 'Country and Irish'. It was formed by mixing American Country music with Irish influences, incorporating Irish folk music. This often resulted in traditional Irish songs being sung in a country music style. It is especially popular in the rural Midlands and North-West of the country. It also remains popular among Irish emigrants in Great Britain. [Big Tom and The Mainliners](/wiki/Big_Tom_and_The_Mainliners "Big Tom and The Mainliners") were the first major contenders in this genre, having crossed over from the showband era of the 1960s. Other major artists were [Philomena Begley](/wiki/Philomena_Begley "Philomena Begley") and [Margo](/wiki/Margo_(singer) "Margo (singer)"), the latter even being bestowed the unofficial title of *Queen of Country & Irish*.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) The most successful performer in the genre today is [Daniel O'Donnell](/wiki/Daniel_O%27Donnell "Daniel O'Donnell"), who has garnered success in the UK, US and Australia.[[34]](#cite_note-34) O'Donnell's frequent singing partner [Mary Duff](/wiki/Mary_Duff "Mary Duff") has also had success in this genre and most recently County Carlow native [Derek Ryan](/wiki/Derek_Ryan_(singer) "Derek Ryan (singer)") has enjoyed Irish chart hits doing this type of music.

### Fusion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Fusion")]

Traditional music played a part in Irish popular music later in the century, with [Clannad](/wiki/Clannad "Clannad"), [Van Morrison](/wiki/Van_Morrison "Van Morrison"), [Hothouse Flowers](/wiki/Hothouse_Flowers "Hothouse Flowers") and [Sinéad O'Connor](/wiki/Sin%C3%A9ad_O%27Connor "Sinéad O'Connor") using traditional elements in popular songs. [Enya](/wiki/Enya "Enya") achieved international success with [New Age](/wiki/New-age_music "New-age music")/Celtic fusions. The [Afro-Celt Sound System](/wiki/Afro-Celt_Sound_System "Afro-Celt Sound System") achieved fame adding West African influences and electronic dance rhythms in the 1990s while bands such as [Kíla](/wiki/K%C3%ADla "Kíla") fuse traditional Irish with rock and world music representing the Irish tradition at world music festivals across Europe and America. The most notable fusion band in Ireland was Horslips, who combined Irish themes and music with heavy rock. [The Shamrock Wings](/w/index.php?title=The_Shamrock_Wings&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Shamrock Wings (page does not exist)") is a Colombian band that fuses Irish music with Caribbean rhythms.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

*[Riverdance](/wiki/Riverdance "Riverdance")* is a musical and dancing interval act which originally starred [Michael Flatley](/wiki/Michael_Flatley "Michael Flatley") and [Jean Butler](/wiki/Jean_Butler "Jean Butler") and featuring the choir [Anúna](/wiki/An%C3%BAna "Anúna"). It was performed during the [Eurovision Song Contest 1994](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_1994 "Eurovision Song Contest 1994") as "[Riverdance](/wiki/Riverdance_(song) "Riverdance (song)")". Popular reaction to the act was so immense that an entire musical revue was built around the act.

### Pop/Rock

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Pop/Rock")]

Main article: [Irish rock](/wiki/Irish_rock "Irish rock")

The 1960s saw the emergence of major Irish rock bands and artists, such as [Them](/wiki/Them_(band) "Them (band)"), [Van Morrison](/wiki/Van_Morrison "Van Morrison"), [Emmet Spiceland](/wiki/Emmet_Spiceland "Emmet Spiceland"), [Eire Apparent](/wiki/Eire_Apparent "Eire Apparent"), [Skid Row](/wiki/Skid_Row_(Irish_band) "Skid Row (Irish band)"), [Taste](/wiki/Taste_(Irish_band) "Taste (Irish band)"), [Rory Gallagher](/wiki/Rory_Gallagher "Rory Gallagher"), [Dr. Strangely Strange](/wiki/Dr._Strangely_Strange "Dr. Strangely Strange"), [Thin Lizzy](/wiki/Thin_Lizzy "Thin Lizzy"), [Gary Moore](/wiki/Gary_Moore "Gary Moore"), [Mellow Candle](/wiki/Mellow_Candle "Mellow Candle").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Front_Thin_Lizzy.jpg/250px-Front_Thin_Lizzy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Front_Thin_Lizzy.jpg)

Thin Lizzy in concert, 1981

In 1970 [Dana](/wiki/Dana_Rosemary_Scallon "Dana Rosemary Scallon") put Ireland on the pop music map by winning the Eurovision Song Contest with her song [All Kinds of Everything](/wiki/All_Kinds_of_Everything "All Kinds of Everything"). She went to number one in the UK and all over Europe and paved the way for many Irish artists. [Gilbert O'Sullivan](/wiki/Gilbert_O%27Sullivan "Gilbert O'Sullivan") went to the top of the charts on both sides of the Atlantic in 1972 with a string of hits, and the all-sister line-up of [The Nolans](/wiki/The_Nolans "The Nolans") gained international chart success in the late 1970s. [Chris de Burgh](/wiki/Chris_de_Burgh "Chris de Burgh") achieved international acclaim with his 1986 hit "[Lady in Red](/wiki/The_Lady_in_Red_(Chris_de_Burgh_song) "The Lady in Red (Chris de Burgh song)")".

Some groups who formed during the emergence of [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") in the mid-late 1970s included [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2"), [Virgin Prunes](/wiki/Virgin_Prunes "Virgin Prunes"), [The Boomtown Rats](/wiki/The_Boomtown_Rats "The Boomtown Rats"), [The Undertones](/wiki/The_Undertones "The Undertones"), [Aslan](/wiki/Aslan_(rock_band) "Aslan (rock band)"), [Gavin Friday](/wiki/Gavin_Friday "Gavin Friday"), and [Stiff Little Fingers](/wiki/Stiff_Little_Fingers "Stiff Little Fingers"). Later in the 80s and into the 90s, Irish punk fractured into new styles of [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock"), which included [That Petrol Emotion](/wiki/That_Petrol_Emotion "That Petrol Emotion"), [In Tua Nua](/wiki/In_Tua_Nua "In Tua Nua"), [Fatima Mansions](/wiki/The_Fatima_Mansions "The Fatima Mansions"), [My Bloody Valentine](/wiki/My_Bloody_Valentine_(band) "My Bloody Valentine (band)") and [Ash](/wiki/Ash_(band) "Ash (band)").[[35]](#cite_note-roc-35) In the 1990s, pop and rock bands like [The Corrs](/wiki/The_Corrs "The Corrs"), [B\*Witched](/wiki/B*Witched "B*Witched"), [Boyzone](/wiki/Boyzone "Boyzone"), [Westlife](/wiki/Westlife "Westlife") and [The Cranberries](/wiki/The_Cranberries "The Cranberries") emerged. In the same decade, Ireland also contributed a subgenre of [folk metal](/wiki/Folk_metal "Folk metal") known as [Celtic metal](/wiki/Celtic_metal "Celtic metal") with exponents of the genre including [Cruachan](/wiki/Cruachan_(band) "Cruachan (band)"), [Primordial](/wiki/Primordial_(band) "Primordial (band)"), [Geasa](/wiki/Geasa_(band) "Geasa (band)"), and [Waylander](/wiki/Waylander_(band) "Waylander (band)").[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Top 5 'most standout' Irish acts of all time

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_of_Ireland&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Top 5 'most standout' Irish acts of all time")]

In 2010, [PRS for Music](/wiki/PRS_for_Music "PRS for Music") conducted research to show which five Irish musicians or [bands](/wiki/Musical_ensemble "Musical ensemble") the public considered to be the 'most standout'. [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2") topped the list with sixty-eight percent[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) while [Westlife](/wiki/Westlife "Westlife"), [Van Morrison](/wiki/Van_Morrison "Van Morrison"), [Boyzone](/wiki/Boyzone "Boyzone") and [The Cranberries](/wiki/The_Cranberries "The Cranberries") came in 2nd, 3rd, 4th and 5th, respectively. The research also suggested that the 'top-five' had sold over 341 million [albums](/wiki/Albums "Albums") up to March 2010.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

| Irish act | Percent | Genre |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1. [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2") | 68 | Rock |
| 2. [Westlife](/wiki/Westlife "Westlife") | 10.5 | Pop |
| 3. [Van Morrison](/wiki/Van_Morrison "Van Morrison") | 10 | Soul |
| 4. [Boyzone](/wiki/Boyzone "Boyzone") | 7.5 | Pop |
| 5. [The Cranberries](/wiki/The_Cranberries "The Cranberries") | 4 | Rock |
